Title: Sucrose Phosphate Synthase Genes in Plants: Its Role and Practice for Crop Improvement
Journal: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SPS is a practical engineering target to increase sucrose accumulation, improve carbon partitioning, yield, fiber quality, and some abiotic stress tolerance. Best deployment should consider family-specific expression, tissue specificity, and promoter choice; stacking with upstream regulators or SPP may be beneficial, while constitutive overexpression may not always be optimal.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher SPS activity generally promotes sucrose accumulation and shifts carbon allocation from starch toward sucrose; can improve growth, yield, quality, and stress tolerance, though effects are species- and family-dependent.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression, antisense suppression/knockdown, knockout mutants, enzyme activity assays, sugar/starch metabolite profiling, correlation analyses across development and genotypes.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light/dark cycles; low temperature; drought; salt stress; cadmium stress; ethylene; auxin; wounding; sugar status (glucose, sucrose); high CO2.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Family- and species-dependent; often high in source leaves, developing or mature sink tissues, and stress-responsive contexts. Examples include leaf-preferential expression in maize and fruit maturation-associated expression in citrus/pear.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not applicable; review synthesizes multiple species.
Common_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not applicable for generic SPS locus
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Phylogenetic sequence collection from NCBI nr database (92 plant SPS sequences, 58 full-length); no unified omics accession reported.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review article. SPS genes are grouped into A/B/C in higher plants, with additional DIII/DIV in grasses. Family-specific biology remains incompletely resolved; C and D family transgenic studies are largely lacking. Some inconsistent phenotypes exist, e.g., OsSPS1 knockout reducing activity without changing sugar ratio in one report, and maize leaf sucrose not always correlating with SPS activity.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive expression may not always generate benefit; outcomes depend on tissue specificity and subcellular context. Reduced SPS causes growth defects and altered starch/sucrose balance. Overexpression may cause off-target shifts in carbon partitioning.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glycosyltransferase-like sucrose-phosphate synthase family enzyme; conserved regulatory phosphoserine motifs including light/dark site, 14-3-3 binding-associated site, and osmotic-stress site.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SPS is the key control enzyme for plant sucrose biosynthesis and carbon partitioning between sucrose and starch. 2. SPS activity is regulated by transcriptional inputs and reversible phosphorylation in response to light/dark and stress cues. 3. Engineering SPS commonly increases sucrose accumulation and can improve yield, biomass, fiber quality, and stress tolerance, but responses are gene-family- and species-dependent.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, stems, fruits, tubers, and other sink tissues depending on species
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Primary photosynthetic transport sugar; carbon allocation; osmoprotection under abiotic stress; supports growth, yield, storage organ filling, and quality traits.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SPS genes are strong metabolic engineering targets for increasing sucrose content and improving yield/quality. Family-specific deployment is recommended; A and B family genes have proven utility, while C and D remain underexplored. Can be paired with SPP or used under source-tissue promoters such as rbcS.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a glucosyl moiety from UDP-glucose to fructose-6-phosphate to produce sucrose-6-phosphate, the immediate precursor of sucrose.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased SPS activity generally elevates sucrose synthesis and often reduces starch accumulation by redirecting carbon flux toward sucrose.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; phylogenetic classification; heterologous overexpression; antisense suppression; knockout/knockdown; SPS enzyme activity assays; sugar and starch quantification.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms the immediate precursor of the target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light/dark; low temperature; drought; salt; cadmium; ethylene; auxin; wounding; glucose and sucrose signaling; high CO2.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Differential among families and tissues; generally high in leaves and in some sucrose-accumulating sink tissues such as maturing fruits and stems.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Varies widely across species and SPS families.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: generic family; see gene-specific entries below
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: NCBI nr sequence retrieval and phylogenetic comparison of 92 SPS sequences (58 full-length); no dataset accession given.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SPS proteins in higher plants carry regulatory phosphoserine motifs absent from bacterial SPS proteins. Light/dark-regulated phosphosites are relatively conserved; D-family proteins show motif divergence at some regulatory sites.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Carbon repartitioning may reduce starch and alter biomass allocation; constitutive expression may produce variable gains depending on tissue and environment.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control enzyme / rate-limiting step widely supported in review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SPS family enzymes catalyze sucrose-6-phosphate formation, a central control point in sucrose biosynthesis. 2. Their expression and phosphorylation status link environmental/developmental inputs to carbon partitioning. 3. Manipulating SPS frequently changes sucrose/starch ratio and agronomic performance.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, fruits, stems, tubers, other sink tissues
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Transport sugar, carbon reserve/distribution molecule, osmoprotectant, growth and yield determinant.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S in transgenic tobacco; endogenous promoter in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum; Populus sp.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AtSPS1 is a validated A-family engineering gene for increasing sucrose and biomass traits in heterologous systems. Useful for biomass crops and fiber/wood improvement, but promoter/tissue specificity should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes sucrose-6-phosphate from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increases sucrose content, plant height, stem diameter, fiber length, and biomass; contributes positively to growth-related carbon allocation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in tobacco and poplar; metabolite and growth trait measurements.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: General growth conditions; seasonal/spring bud sprouting and senescence effects observed in poplar.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not detailed here for Arabidopsis AtSPS1 specifically; Arabidopsis SPS genes are differentially expressed and loss of SPSa1/SPSc impairs growth.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Arabidopsis source plant; specific pattern not detailed in this review for AtSPS1 alone.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: SPP (at SPS/SPP interaction level in planta)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified in family A. Arabidopsis has four SPS genes total, with two in family A.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not specifically reported; general caution that constitutive SPS expression may not always confer gains in every context.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step supported by review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtSPS1 is an A-family SPS gene used successfully in transgenic engineering. 2. Its overexpression in tobacco and poplar increases sucrose-associated growth and fiber traits. 3. These results support SPS-mediated improvement of biomass and carbon allocation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and stems; whole-plant transport pools
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Carbon transport, biomass formation, support of growth and structural carbon deposition.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S in rice; endogenous promoter in rice studies
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OsSPS1 is a promising target for rice yield improvement, especially source-leaf carbon supply during transplanting and spike formation. Could be deployed through expression enhancement in source tissues; caution because some studies found weak effects on sugar ratio after knockout.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Produces sucrose-6-phosphate from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Suppression decreases plant height; enhanced expression/activity is associated with increased SPS activity in source leaves, higher spikelet number per panicle, increased height, yield, and dry matter. However, one knockout report found reduced SPS activity without altered starch/sucrose ratio or overall growth.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Knockout/suppression analyses; association of endogenous activity with agronomic traits; transgenic expression of maize SPS in rice for comparison.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Drought stress increases SPS activity and sucrose in stems; developmental stages such as transplanting and spike formation are relevant.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Rice has five SPS genes in distinct families; OsSPS1-associated activity is notable in source leaf during transplanting and spike formation stages.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rice endogenous expression; specific tissue details beyond source leaves/stages not fully given.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Rice genome contains five SPS genes, each in a distinct family. Reported functional effects of OsSPS1 are somewhat inconsistent across studies.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential context dependence; knockout may not always change sugar ratio; overexpression needs source-sink optimization.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step supported, but locus-specific dominance not fully established
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsSPS1 contributes to rice growth and yield-related carbon allocation. 2. Reduced expression lowers plant height, while higher source-leaf SPS activity correlates with more spikelets, yield, and dry matter. 3. Some reports show reduced SPS activity without sugar-ratio changes, indicating functional redundancy or context dependence.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and stems; transported to panicles

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: rbcS in tomato, Arabidopsis, tobacco; 35S in potato and rice
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Solanum lycopersicum; Solanum tuberosum; Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ZmSPS1 is the most validated SPS transgene for crop improvement. It can enhance source strength, fruit sugar, fruit number, ripening, tuber yield, cold tolerance, and plant stature. Best used with source-tissue promoters like rbcS or constitutive 35S depending target trait.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes sucrose-6-phosphate formation from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression strongly increases SPS activity, elevates sucrose/starch ratio, increases sucrose export and fruit sucrose, reduces leaf starch accumulation, promotes fruit number and earlier ripening, increases potato yield, enhances Arabidopsis cold tolerance, and increases rice height.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Multiple transgenic overexpression studies with rbcS or 35S promoters; SPS activity assays; sugar/starch measurements; yield and developmental trait analyses under ambient and high CO2 or low temperature.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High CO2; low temperature; normal cultivation conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In maize, ZmSPS1 is highly expressed in leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as family B. It is the most extensively used SPS gene in transgenic studies summarized in this review.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can reduce starch accumulation in leaves; total dry matter did not always change despite altered partitioning in some tomato experiments.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmSPS1 overexpression robustly enhances SPS activity and drives carbon partitioning toward sucrose. 2. In tomato it increases fruit number, sucrose content, ripening speed, and total dry weight while reducing leaf starch. 3. In potato, Arabidopsis, tobacco, and rice it improves yield-related traits or stress tolerance, making it a premier SPS engineering gene.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, fruits, tubers, transported assimilate pools
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Carbon transport, osmoprotection, yield and quality determination.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SoSPS1 is a useful endogenous B-family transgene for increasing sucrose accumulation in sugarcane, with added benefits for height and stem number in some lines. Suitable for sugar-content improvement programs.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes sucrose-6-phosphate synthesis from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increases SPS activity and sucrose content in leaves and stems, with higher glucose and fructose and in some lines increased plant height and stem number.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Sugarcane transgenic overexpression; enzyme activity assays; sugar quantification.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: General cultivation conditions; family-level discussion also notes light/dark regulation and phosphorylation sites in sugarcane SPS proteins.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Sugarcane SPS family genes show diverse expression patterns in leaves and stems across studies.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as family B. Distinct from cloned sugarcane genes SofSPSA/B/DIII listed in Table 1.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible increases in hexoses indicate broader carbohydrate repartitioning; line-specific outcomes expected.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SoSPS1 overexpression increases SPS activity and sucrose content in sugarcane. 2. It also raises glucose and fructose levels and can improve plant height and stem number in some lines. 3. It is a strong candidate for sugarcane sugar-yield engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Primary stored/transported sugar supporting sugar yield and osmotic adjustment.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SolSPS can be used to improve fiber quality and maturation by enhancing sucrose supply and secondary wall thickening. Relevant for fiber crops where carbon allocation to cell wall biosynthesis is desired.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes formation of sucrose-6-phosphate from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Transgenic cotton shows a higher sucrose/starch ratio, increased micronaire value and maturity rate, secondary cell wall thickening in fiber cells, and improved quality.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous overexpression in cotton with biochemical and fiber-quality phenotyping.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not detailed in review for transgenic cotton; source spinach gene used heterologously.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Spinach SPS expression increases from leaf elongation to maturity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as family A. Spinach SPS also served as a key model for light/dark and osmotic-stress phosphoregulation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May repartition carbon away from starch; otherwise no specific penalty reported here.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SolSPS increases sucrose availability in transgenic cotton. 2. Enhanced sucrose partitioning promotes fiber secondary wall thickening and quality improvement. 3. Spinach SPS is also a classic model for SPS phosphoregulation studies.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Carbon supply for growth and structural polysaccharide deposition.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CitSPS1 is a promising fruit sugar-improvement target for citrus breeding due to strong association with fruit sucrose accumulation during maturation. Could be combined with maturation-stage promoters or fruit-specific editing/upregulation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes sucrose-6-phosphate formation from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression in edible tissues throughout fruit development, peaking at maturity, is associated with sucrose synthesis/accumulation in citrus fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Developmental expression profiling and correlation with fruit sucrose accumulation/SPS activity.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit developmental stage; maturity at 223 days after flowering.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in edible tissues throughout fruit development, highest at maturity.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not specified in text for CitSPS1
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Family A SPS gene in citrus.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as important contributor, key control not proven alone
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CitSPS1 is expressed continuously in citrus edible tissues during fruit development. 2. Its transcript peaks at fruit maturity. 3. The pattern supports a major role in fruit sucrose synthesis and accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CitSPS2 is a candidate for enhancing late-stage citrus fruit sweetness; may be especially useful for maturation-stage regulation rather than constitutive expression.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes sucrose-6-phosphate formation from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Late induction in edible tissues followed by peak expression at maturity suggests a significant role in late fruit sucrose synthesis/accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Developmental expression profiling in fruit tissues correlated with sucrose accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit development stage; absent before 187 days after flowering, peaks at 223 days.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not expressed in edible tissues early; induced after 187 DAF and highest at maturity.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not specified in text for CitSPS2
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Family B SPS gene in citrus.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as important contributor, key control not proven alone
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CitSPS2 is a late-expressed citrus SPS gene in edible fruit tissues. 2. Its induction near maturation implicates it in terminal fruit sucrose accumulation. 3. It likely complements CitSPS1 in determining citrus sweetness.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: NtSPSC highlights family-specific specialization and may be useful for engineering sucrose synthesis under nonphotosynthetic conditions or heterotrophic tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes sucrose-6-phosphate formation from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to starch transport under nonphotosynthetic conditions, thereby supporting sucrose synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional interpretation from family-specific expression/function summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Associated with nonphotosynthetic contexts in tobacco.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Core sucrose synthesis step in heterotrophic context
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tobacco C-family SPS gene; review notes C- and D-family biology remains much less explored than A/B families.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NtSPSC is a C-family SPS gene with distinct functional specialization. 2. It participates in starch transport/remobilization under nonphotosynthetic conditions. 3. This supports family-specific functional divergence among plant SPS genes.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ChSPS1 is a candidate for fruit sweetness improvement in pear and can function heterologously to raise sucrose and SPS activity. Useful for fruit sugar breeding.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes sucrose-6-phosphate formation from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Shows broad tissue expression with peak in mature fruit; expression positively correlates with sucrose content. Overexpression in tobacco raises SPS activity and sucrose content.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling and correlation in pear; heterologous overexpression in tobacco.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in all tissues, highest in mature fruits.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Pear gene name presented as ChSPS1 in review text; likely from European pear study summarized therein.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as important contributor, not individually proven as sole bottleneck
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ChSPS1 expression peaks in mature pear fruits and correlates positively with sucrose content. 2. Ectopic expression in tobacco increases SPS activity and sucrose content. 3. The gene is a promising target for fruit sweetness improvement.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SPSA2 affects drought-related carbon metabolism and may be a specialized target for stress-adaptive sucrose management rather than bulk sucrose enhancement alone.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes sucrose-6-phosphate formation from UDP-glucose and fructose-6-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of sucrose phosphate synthase A2 approximately halves total soluble sugars and fructose relative to wild type; involved in drought response via activation of the plastid oxidative pentose phosphate pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant/loss-of-function analysis under drought-related context; metabolite measurement.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of sucrose and supports stress-responsive carbon metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Described in review as 'lack of sucrose phosphate synthase A2' reducing soluble sugars and fructose in Arabidopsis; also linked to drought via plastid oxidative pentose phosphate pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss reduces soluble sugar pools, potentially compromising growth/stress responses.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SPSA2 contributes to sugar accumulation in Arabidopsis. 2. Its loss lowers soluble sugars and fructose. 3. It also participates in drought-responsive metabolic adjustment via the plastid oxidative pentose phosphate pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and whole-plant soluble sugar pools

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: 14-3-3-mediated control of SPS phosphorylation could be exploited to tune SPS activity without changing coding sequence abundance; useful for condition-dependent sucrose engineering if specific interactions can be manipulated.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Binding of 14-3-3 proteins to phosphorylated SPS is associated with negative post-translational regulation of SPS activity.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phosphosite mapping and protein interaction evidence cited in review; identification of Ser229 as 14-3-3 interaction-associated phosphorylation site.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller that modulates SPS activity and therefore sucrose synthesis through phosphorylation-dependent protein binding.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light/dark and possibly osmotic/stress contexts affecting SPS phosphorylation state.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes conservation/divergence of the 14-3-3 binding-associated site across SPS families; D family often has serine-to-leucine replacement at this site.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating broad 14-3-3 signaling may cause pleiotropic effects because these proteins regulate many pathways.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms SPS–14-3-3 regulatory complex upon phosphorylation-associated binding
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Protein binding to phosphorylated target; post-translational inhibition/modulation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Binds phosphorylated SPS and negatively modulates enzyme activity
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SPS activity is modulated post-translationally through phosphorylation-dependent 14-3-3 interaction. 2. A conserved SPS phosphorylation site is linked to 14-3-3 binding. 3. This module provides a rapid mechanism to adjust sucrose synthesis under changing conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, stems, fruits, tubers depending on species
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Transport sugar, osmotic regulator, yield and quality determinant.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Light/dark cues; phosphorylation state of SPS; stress signals

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Protein phosphatase-based activation of SPS may be leveraged to enhance daytime sucrose synthesis and optimize circadian carbon partitioning; however, specificity is essential to avoid broad pleiotropy.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: A circadian-regulated protein phosphatase promotes SPS dephosphorylation, corresponding to higher SPS activity and enhanced daytime sucrose synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Circadian correlation of protein phosphatase transcription with SPS phosphorylation/activity state as summarized in review; okadaic acid sensitivity supports phosphatase involvement.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent activator of sucrose production through dephosphorylation and activation of SPS.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light/daytime; circadian rhythm; sensitivity to okadaic acid.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Circadian control implied; direct feedback not established.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific phosphatase identity not provided in the review text.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating phosphatases broadly could affect many signaling pathways.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: SPS phosphoserine sites involved in light/dark regulation
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts on SPS possibly within signaling complexes; not specifically defined
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates SPS enzyme by dephosphorylation rather than transcriptional control
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SPS is most active in its dephosphorylated state during daytime. 2. A circadian-regulated protein phosphatase is implicated in establishing rhythmic SPS activity. 3. This links clock/light inputs to sucrose biosynthesis capacity.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Photosynthetic tissues and downstream sink organs
